lines: •Confidentiality covers•Data confidentiality: sensitive information is available to authorized parties only•Privacy: individuals can control what information about them can be collected and stored and to whom it is made available•Integrity covers•Data integrity: information and software can be modified only in a predetermined and authorized manner•System integrity: a system performs its intended functions in an expected manner and has not be manipulated in an unauthorized way•Availability: Assures that systems work promptly and service is not denied to authorized usersSecurity Objectives

lines: •Authenticity: the property of being genuine and being able to be verified and trusted•entity authentication: the entity is who it claims it is•data authentication: the data is coming from a trusted source•Access control: only authorized parties can use specific resources incompliance with their privileges•Non-repudiability(repudiability): inability (ability) to deny communication or actions•Accountability: the requirement that all actions of an entity are traced uniquely to that entity•covers non-repudiability, intrusion detection, fault isolation, and etc.More on Security Objectives

lines: •Potential attacks on the security features need to be considered•Procedures used to provide particular services are often counter-intuitive•It is necessary to decide where to use the various security mechanisms•Requires constantmonitoring•Attackers only need to find a single weakness, the developer needs to find all weaknesses•Users and system managers tend to NOT see the benefits of security until a failure occurs•…Security is not Simple

lines: •Assets can have different security grades depending on the impact of a security breach that can range from low to high•By building more secure systems, we make it harder for an attacker to breach security•The more resources we can invest in a system, the more secure we can make it•There is a trade-off between security and resources (money, equipment, personnel, training)•Training must cover all users, as security can often be easiest breached by exploiting human errorSecurity is not Absolute

lines: •Passive: observes information without intervention•e.g., passively monitoring a communication link•Active: alters system resources or affects their operation•e.g., changing messages, replaying old messages on the network, corrupting users, etc.•Insider: is legitimately a part of the system with access to internal data or is inside the security perimeter•Outsider: is outside of the security perimeter or is not a legitimate userTypes of Adversaries

lines: •Policy defines “security” for the site, system, etc.•composition of policies is non-trivial•if policies conflict, discrepancies can create security vulnerabilities•Mechanismsenforce policies•Security goals w.r.t. policies•Prevention: prevent attackers from violating security policy•Detection: detect attacks’ violation of security policy•Recovery: stop attack, assess and repair damage; continue to function even if attack succeedsPolicies and Mechanisms

lines: •The WiFi standard lacks good mechanisms to mitigate interference, especially in dense AP deployments•Multiple access protocol (CSMA/CD) from 1970s•Static channel assignment, power levels, and carrier sensing thresholds•In such deployments WiFi systems exhibit poor spectrum reuse and significant contention among APs and clients•Result is low throughput and a poor user experience•MU-MIMO will help each AP, but not interfering APsWhy does WiFi performance suck?
Carrier Sense Multiple Access: if another WiFi signal detected, random backoff Collision Detection: if collisiondetected, resend

4
Lecture 1: Course Introduction
•Course: Fundamentalof Wireless Network and IoT Security•Time: 13:25–15:50(Lecture, Thursday), 16:15–17:50(Lab, Thursday)•Classroom: 7thTeaching Building –Room 104(Lecture)West CaoguangbiaoBuilding –Room 501 (Lab)•Prerequisite: None•Teaching Assistant: JianwenZhou(周建文), jianwenz@zju.edu.cnCourse Overview

5
Lecture 1: Course Introduction
•Major in Information Security,•Major in Computer Science and Technology, •and•are familiar with basic computer networks and discrete mathematics,•are preparing to obtain fundamentals and recent developments in the wireless and Internet of Things (IoT) security.Target Students

6
Lecture 1: Course Introduction
•Textbook:•1. Security and Cooperation in Wireless Networks, by LeventeButtyanand Jean-Pierre Hubaux, Cambridge University Press, ISBN 9780521873710, 2007•2. Cryptography and Network Security: Principles and Practice, Seventh Edition, by William Stallings, Pearson Education, 2017•and also many recently published papers (will be delivered)Resources

7
Lecture 1: Course Introduction
•Lectures•Labs (Total 3)•One Lab for Two Weeks, except the first and last week•One Final ExamTeaching

9
Lecture 1: Course Introduction
•Academic integrity: lifeline in academia•Your labsand exams must be your own -we have a zero tolerance policy towards cheating of any kind and any student who cheats will get•Zerofor the project/exam for the course •Both the cheater and the person(s) who aided the cheater will be held responsible for the cheatingAcademic Integrity Policy

10
Lecture 1: Course Introduction
•Lectures do not cover everything•Not all questions in exam are from lectures•But many of them are J•Your responsibility•Always attend lectures •Carefully readtextbook, lecture notes, assigned papers •Actively ask questions•Well prepare for the examsLecture Format

11
Lecture 1: Course Introduction
•Fundamentals of Wireless Communication and IoT•Wireless Network Security (WiFi/ Cellular Mobile Network)•Near Field Sensing and Security (RFID, NFC)•Bluetooth Security and Localization Security (GPS, UWB)•IoT Security (Fundamentals, Accessibility, System Security)•Other Related Topics (Side channel/ Anonymous communication)•Due to the time limits, we will try to cover most of the major topics without going into too much detail.Table of Contents

12
Lecture 1: Course Introduction
Now Let’s Start from Security!

13
Lecture 1: Course Introduction
•Security (failures) are constantly on the news•Malware Infections•Ransomware•Data Breaches•Espionage•Cyberwar•Everyone is affected•Individuals•Corporations•GovernmentsWhat is Security?

14
Lecture 1: Course Introduction
•Some scary numbers•1 in 13 web requests lead to malware•Spam accounts for 55% of email•An average user receives dozens of viruses in email per year•Credit card numbers are sold for $0.5 -$25•Hacked gmailaccounts sell for $0.1 -$5•Malware can be as cheap a $1•Identity (name, SSN, DOB) goes for $0.1 -$1.5Data Breaches are Increasingly Common

15
Lecture 1: Course Introduction
•The NIST Computer Security Handbook defines the term Computer Security as: “The protection afforded to an automated information system in order to attain the applicable objectives of preserving the integrity, availabilityand confidentialityof information system resources”  (includes hardware, software, firmware, information/data, and telecommunications)What is Security on Earth?

16
Lecture 1: Course Introduction
•Confidentiality covers•Data confidentiality: sensitive information is available to authorized parties only•Privacy: individuals can control what information about them can be collected and stored and to whom it is made available•Integrity covers•Data integrity: information and software can be modified only in a predetermined and authorized manner•System integrity: a system performs its intended functions in an expected manner and has not be manipulated in an unauthorized way•Availability: Assures that systems work promptly and service is not denied to authorized usersSecurity Objectives

17
Lecture 1: Course Introduction
•Authenticity: the property of being genuine and being able to be verified and trusted•entity authentication: the entity is who it claims it is•data authentication: the data is coming from a trusted source•Access control: only authorized parties can use specific resources incompliance with their privileges•Non-repudiability(repudiability): inability (ability) to deny communication or actions•Accountability: the requirement that all actions of an entity are traced uniquely to that entity•covers non-repudiability, intrusion detection, fault isolation, and etc.More on Security Objectives

18
Lecture 1: Course Introduction
•The means of achieving these objectives greatly differ•Cryptographic techniques•Access control policies•Software checking tools•Spam filters, etc.•Each system must be evaluated uniquelyin terms of its requirements•Security mechanisms must be adequately chosen in accordance with those requirementsHow do We Achieve These Goals?

19
Lecture 1: Course Introduction
•Potential attacks on the security features need to be considered•Procedures used to provide particular services are often counter-intuitive•It is necessary to decide where to use the various security mechanisms•Requires constantmonitoring•Attackers only need to find a single weakness, the developer needs to find all weaknesses•Users and system managers tend to NOT see the benefits of security until a failure occurs•…Security is not Simple

20
Lecture 1: Course Introduction
•Assets can have different security grades depending on the impact of a security breach that can range from low to high•By building more secure systems, we make it harder for an attacker to breach security•The more resources we can invest in a system, the more secure we can make it•There is a trade-off between security and resources (money, equipment, personnel, training)•Training must cover all users, as security can often be easiest breached by exploiting human errorSecurity is not Absolute

21
Lecture 1: Course Introduction
•Adversary•Attack•Countermeasure•Risk•Security Policy•System Resource•Threat•VulnerabilityComputer Security Terminology

22
Lecture 1: Course Introduction
•Passive: observes information without intervention•e.g., passively monitoring a communication link•Active: alters system resources or affects their operation•e.g., changing messages, replaying old messages on the network, corrupting users, etc.•Insider: is legitimately a part of the system with access to internal data or is inside the security perimeter•Outsider: is outside of the security perimeter or is not a legitimate userTypes of Adversaries
